title: SVO Sentence
description: Master the most common Finnish sentence pattern: Subject + Verb + Object, and understand object cases (partitive, nominative, genitive)

questions.explanation: SVO = Subject-Verb-Object. This is the basic sentence structure: Who (subject) + What do they do (verb) + What do they act on (object).

questions.text: Identify the subject, verb, and object in: 'Lisa ostaa kirjan' (Lisa buys a book).
questions.explanation: Lisa (subject) = who? ostaa (verb) = does what? kirjan (object) = acts on what? This is the SVO pattern.

options: A verb that can have an object (someone or something receives the action)
A verb that has many syllables
questions.explanation: Transitive verbs can have objects. Example: ostaa (buy) can have an object—you buy SOMETHING. Intransitive verbs cannot: olla (to be) cannot have an object.

questions.text: Which of these is an INTRANSITIVE verb (cannot have an object)?
questions.explanation: 'Asua' is intransitive—you don't 'live' SOMETHING. You live IN a place (uses local case). The others are transitive and can have objects.

questions.text: When should you use the PARTITIVE case for the object?
options: Only when the subject is negative
When the sentence is negative, or the object is indefinite/uncountable, or the verb requires partitive
questions.explanation: Partitive is used for: negative sentences, indefinite quantities (uncountable), and partitive verbs (opiskella, tahtoa). Example: En osta kirjaa (I don't buy a book).

questions.text: Why is 'Luen kirjaa' (I'm reading a book) in partitive, not genitive?
questions.explanation: The action is NOT finished—you're in the middle of reading. Partitive shows the action is incomplete. When finished: 'Luin kirjan' (genitive).

questions.text: When do you use NOMINATIVE case for the object?
options: For imperative (commands), passive voice, and necessity structures
questions.explanation: Nominative is used in commands (Osta kirja!), passive (Ostetaan kirja), and necessity (Täytyy ostaa kirja). But partitive can override these rules!

questions.text: Write the imperative (command): 'Buy the book!' in Finnish. What case is the object?
questions.explanation: Osta (buy!) + kirja (nominative). But if you said 'Osta kahta kirjaa!' (Buy two books), the partitive takes over because of the number.

questions.text: When do you use GENITIVE case for the object?
options: For actions that are finished/completed, or countable whole items
questions.explanation: Genitive is the 'default' for finished actions and countable items. 'Luin kirjan' (I read the book—finished). 'Ostan auton' (I buy a car—one whole car).

questions.text: Translate: 'I washed the car.' What case is the object and why?
questions.explanation: 'Pesin auton' uses genitive because the action is FINISHED—the car is now clean. Genitive indicates completed action with a clear result.

questions.text: Which sentence correctly uses the object case?
options: Mä opiskelen suomea (I study Finnish - partitive)
Mä opiskelen suomen (I study Finnish - genitive)
Mä opiskelen suomi (I study Finnish - nominative)
questions.explanation: 'Opiskella' is a partitive verb, so it ALWAYS requires partitive, even without other reasons. 'Mä opiskelen suomea' is correct.

questions.text: Explain why 'Otan kahvia' (I take coffee) uses partitive instead of genitive.
questions.explanation: 'Kahvi' is uncountable (a mass noun). You can't say 'three coffees' in the same way. Indefinite quantity = partitive. 'Otan kahvia' = I take some coffee.

questions.text: What case should the object be in this sentence? 'Ei osta kirja_' (Doesn't buy a book)
questions.explanation: Negative sentences ALWAYS use partitive: 'Ei osta kirjaa.' This is one of the strongest partitive rules—negative wins over everything!

questions.text: Create 3 SVO sentences with different object cases. Explain why each case is used.
questions.explanation: Example answers: 'En juo kahvia' (partitive=negative), 'Joi kahvin' (genitive=finished action), 'Juon kahvia' (partitive=ongoing/habitual).
